text:
It is unlikely that anyone in our provincial town will forget the hard-working boxer who once trained behind the old cathedral. He was born in a solitary brick house near the airport, the son of a gardener, and from an early age he had an absolute, heartfelt motive: to escape the dropout's fate and make something terrific of his life. His physique was ungainly at first, chiefly because a childhood surgery had left one shoulder stiff, but he learned to pronounce each ambition aloud, dwelling on every vowel as if the very sound were a referent for his dream, a quiet creed of humanism that put human effort above all. Years of experience, stacked one upon the next like a tall stack of worn gloves, would standardize his own resolve.

He would bypass the noisy cafeteria and ride his bicycle past the zebra crossing to a humble gym above a kitchen that always smelled of butter and toast. There he would devote whole afternoons to the chase of skill, working a single tactic until it ran smoothly. A bossy old coach, a former commissioner of the regional league, did not spare the rebuke; his harsh words could seize a man's pride, yet they were never pure cruelty. 'Comply with the protocol,' he would say, 'or simply leave.'

The coach loved to elaborate. His elaboration on footwork was a kind of verse, and he could compile a whole canon of drills from memory. He taught the boy to understand each facet of the ring, to contrast a clumsy lunge with a clean one, and to ascertain an opponent's weakness before the first horn. 'Hatred has no place here,' he said. 'Neutrality of mind, and a fruitful patience, will produce the win.' It was a fallacy, he insisted, that rage alone could shoot a man to victory; that was an awful and prohibitive lie.

Life outside the gym was harder. Inflation had made even postage dear, and the cost of clean linen and good fabric for his robe was nearly prohibitive. A provincial reporter, eager for an issue to fill the page, once wrote a doubtful piece that seemed to trespass on his privacy, citing a faked facsimile of a contract. The boy felt mean and blue for a day, then would refresh his focus and let the slight pass like a breeze.

On Boxing-Day, the eleven-year mark of his career and his eleventh fight, he stepped onto the stage. The hall, thick with smog from outside and the heat of the crowd, would hinge on a single round. He would not hurl wild blows; he moved in a calm orbit, a compound of speed and grace, until the executive judges, with absolute fairness, named him the winner. A typhoon of cheers rose. Royalty itself, they joked, would envy such comfort and delight. He sipped a cold beverage, qualify at last for the national stage, and offered a quiet specimen of thanks, a delicacy of feeling that no isolation or fame could ever take from him.
translation:
我们这座外省小镇上，大概没有人会忘记那位曾在老教堂后面训练的勤奋拳手。他出生在机场附近一栋孤零零的砖房里，是一位园丁的儿子，从小他就怀着一个绝对而真挚的动机：摆脱辍学者的命运，让自己的人生有所成就。起初他的体格笨拙，但他学会把每一个志向大声说出来，仿佛要把自己的决心规范化。

他会绕开喧闹的食堂，骑着自行车经过斑马线，来到一间厨房楼上的简陋拳馆，那厨房里总飘着黄油和烤面包的味道。在那里他会把整个下午奉献给对技艺的追逐，反复练一个战术，直到它运转流畅。一位专横的老教练——地区联赛的前任专员——从不吝惜责备；他刻薄的话能攫住一个人的自尊，但那从不是纯粹的残忍。'遵守规程，'他会说，'否则干脆走人。'

教练喜欢详加阐述。他对步法的阐述像是一种韵文，他能凭记忆编纂出一整套训练教规。他教这男孩理解拳台的每一个方面，把笨拙的出击与利落的出击作对比，并在第一声号角响起前就探明对手的弱点。'这里没有仇恨的位置，'他说。'心境的中立，加上富有成效的耐心，才能赢下比赛。'他坚称，以为单凭狂怒就能把一个人射向胜利，那是一种谬误；那是个可怕而要命的谎言。

拳馆之外的生活更艰难。通货膨胀让连邮费都变贵，做拳袍要用的干净亚麻和好布料几乎贵得离谱。一位急于找个话题填版面的外省记者，曾写过一篇可疑的报道，似乎侵犯了他的隐私，还引用了一份伪造的合同传真件。男孩沮丧消沉了一天，随后重整专注，让这点轻慢像微风一样掠过。

在节礼日，他职业生涯的第十一场比赛中，他登上了擂台。大厅里弥漫着外面飘来的烟雾和人群的热气，胜负系于一个回合。他不会胡乱挥拳；他在一个沉稳的轨道里移动，是速度与优雅的结合，直到执行裁判们以绝对的公正宣布他获胜。一阵欢呼如台风般涌起。他们打趣说，连王室都会羡慕这样的舒适与喜悦。他啜了一口冰凉的饮料，终于有资格登上全国舞台，并献上一份安静的谢意样本，一种任何孤独或名声都夺不走的细腻情感。
